Completes critical safety checks before prescribing: asks about drug allergies, reviews current medications/substances for interactions (including alcohol, opioids, other sedatives), and checks contraindications (pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory disease/OSA risk, older adult fall risk, history of substance use disorder as applicable to chosen agent).

Provides clear patient-centered instructions (how/when to take, avoid driving/alcohol, what to do if next-day sedation occurs) and sets expectations for duration and discontinuation/taper plan when relevant.
Creates a follow-up recommendation in narrative (even if not using a tool) including timeframe to reassess sleep, side effects, adherence to CBT-I, and need to reconsider diagnosis if no improvement.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, recommend CBT-I as first-line, and (if needed) prescribe a short-term insomnia medication after completing allergy/interaction/contraindication checks.
must_collect: sleep complaint type (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening)
sleep apnea red flags (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness)
history of falls/cognitive impairment (older adults) if relevant
steps: Elicit insomnia pattern, duration, and daytime consequences; confirm adequate opportunity for sleep.
Screen for secondary causes/comorbid sleep disorders and address modifiable contributors (substances, meds, pain, stress).
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and whether acute vs chronic.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; provide practical CBT-I elements and resources.
If prescribing: perform allergy check, medication/substance interaction check, and contraindication check; select an appropriate short-term agent when indicated; counsel on risks and safe use.
Advise follow-up timeframe (e.g., 2–4 weeks) to reassess response, side effects, and adherence; escalate evaluation if red flags or poor response.

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I only (no pharmacotherapy) after diagnosing insomnia and ruling out urgent mimics; provides structured behavioral plan and follow-up recommendation.
steps: Assess insomnia characteristics, duration, daytime impairment, and sleep opportunity.
Screen for contributors/mimics (substances/meds, mood disorders, RLS, OSA red flags).
Record diagnosis of insomnia.
Provide CBT-I-focused plan with stimulus control, sleep restriction guidance, and sleep hygiene reinforcement.
Recommend follow-up in 2–4 weeks and earlier if worsening mood/suicidality, severe daytime sleepiness, or OSA concerns.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication plus CBT-I with emphasis on short-term use and deprescribing plan; chooses a safer option based on patient factors (e.g., avoids sedatives in high fall-risk/OSA risk).
steps: Confirm insomnia diagnosis and assess severity/functional impact.
Discuss CBT-I as first-line and start behavioral plan.
Before prescribing, complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks including alcohol/other sedatives and OSA risk.
Prescribe an appropriate short-term insomnia medication when benefits outweigh risks; provide safety counseling and duration limits.
Plan reassessment in 1–2 weeks for side effects and 2–4 weeks for efficacy; outline discontinuation/taper if applicable.
